replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith   making bumps on the inside piece will cause more friction too.  mine is smooth though.  don't want to create steam.  Don't want to build to much pressure up.   we blew a big one up already . can't be sealed.

author: @erlendse

a kinda simple question: What will be the source of rotational power in the final jig?
Electricity would seem silly, since a electric heater of some form would work better compared to a motor spinning the heater.

author: @rheandros
text:
Wow just something i really worked on. Friction heater needs min. 3600U/min.
I used 6000U  with 2kW a big angle grinder the small one, smoked a little bit.
6min made 120°C in the oil and the Housing
The inner and outer pipe should be electrical seperated (f.e. wood) i used disks 4mm thick, to thick i think 3mm would do it. 10w40 was my choice of oil. My dad said try hydraulik oil
There was tests with this heater and an resistor heater no difference.
Oil piggys design will be different he said. But i dont testet it. The plan is too difficould for me.
If you get it to work please share youre work

Peace truth love

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: No worries mate - i will do - but i am not expecting to have such power requirements = there is no real need for electric separation that I am aware of. As far as I understand it it is a boundary value problem so surface area is an issue - but i plan on an everyman oilpiggy - it will go up as a make it guide if i manage it

author: @EgadsNo
text:
Have you considered using copper to copper friction?  Has a very high thermal conductivity so it can draw heat away quickly to prevent seizing provided the stationary chunk is of suitable mass or directly heatsinked to what you want your black body emitter to be.  Which really should be something that transfers heat poorly but has a high density so it can hold a large amount of specific heat and limits heating the air-  returning more as blackbody infrared.

Another key feature to direct contact you could adjust the torque to the speed.  WIth something like a thrust bearing and a spring, with a adjustable mounting for the bearing.  You could then tune the machine to work efficiently with low speed high torque (direct human powered) or high speed low torque drivetrains.

The oil I must say is pretty elegant of a solution though in terms of wear and tear maintenance but seems to suffer a bit of inflexibility on power input.  I actually bet copper would hold up quite well of suitable gauge it has a surprising tensile strength for its malleability, the spring and thrust bearing though might be the big weak links.   I'd love to try it myself but would likely go a completely different route and shoot for electrical resistance as the energy conversion- plus I have a ton of other ideas I have to get too, particularly the ones I have intended for the new vacuum pump my wife is concerned to why I bought ;)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Copper is a bit expensive - I am just looking - I am doing an ultra cheap and then a disk and then a cavitation type - that seems like enough!! But keep me up to date with what you are up to and i'd love to see the vids

replies.author: @victoryfirst2878
replies.text: I agree with you EgadsNo using the copper for the thermal conductivity. What I am wondering is the actual  efficiency of this type of unit. Would you have some numbers of range ?? Now I am wondering what the vacuum pump will be used for Sir ??? Good day Sir.

author: @kennedy67951
text: Hey Robert this system you just built is used to heat water. Some Fire departments here in the USA use this as a cheaper way to heat water. Small home systems are available here too.  I just thought I would let you know.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @actudoran
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith before I heard the description of the thingie I thought you were going to build a cavitation heater too.  - PS ... my auto correct dictionary doesn't know "cavitation" and "thingie" :)) 
multiple discs parallel to each other seem like a multi layered closed version of a Tesla Turbine :) 
can't wait to see the efficiency differential ... somehow I think I know what goes on and how to improove on it ... ok ... we got the sigma ( superficial tension coefficient ) of oil and pipe ... we multiply that by two and that's your gap at which you should have max interraction between oil clinging to inside and outside pipe ... 
Simpler method ... measure the "rise" of oil to the pipe "boundary thickness" ... x 2 ... bob's our uncle ... that's the gap :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: actudoran looked like a closed tesla to me too - thanks for the hints mate
